---
title: "Compose distributions into event trees"
description: "Wire delays into a tree structure with Sequential, Parallel, Resolve, and Compete — one object that simulates and scores a record."
toc: true
toc-location: left
---

:::::: {.side-space}

::: {.section-start-space}

## The composed way of thinking

A case's progression through detection and care is a tree of delays. A single
composed distribution describes the whole record: it simulates a case's event
path with `rand` and scores an observed record with `logpdf`.

The [Bundibugyo Ebola line-list analysis](https://censoreddistributions.epiaware.org/dev/getting-started/tutorials/bdbv-linelist-analysis/)
is the north-star example — it builds one composed tree for onset-to-admission,
admission-to-resolution (death vs discharge), and onset-to-notification, fits
it to real data, and recovers the published delay estimates.

The code window below builds a similar tree: two parallel pathways — clinical
and surveillance — each a sequential chain with a disjunction inside.

:::

::: {.code-example-column .section-start-space .section-end-space}
::: {.code-window .box}
::: {.code-window-header}
::: {.code-window-dots}

```{=html}
<span class="dot red"></span><span class="dot yellow"></span><span class="dot green"></span>
```

:::

::: {.code-window-title}

```{=html}
<span>composed_delays.jl</span>
```

:::

:::

```{.julia .code-overflow-scroll}
using ComposedDistributions, Distributions

# BDBV-inspired delay tree: parallel pathways
# with Sequential, Resolve, and Compete nested together
tree = compose((
    clinical = sequential(
        :onset_admit => Gamma(1.2, 3.0),
        :admit_resolve => resolve(
            :death => (Gamma(2.0, 3.5), 0.3),
            :discharge => (Gamma(1.0, 8.0), 0.7))),
    surveillance = sequential(
        :onset_notif => Gamma(0.7, 20.0),
        :notif_compete => compete(
            :confirmed => Gamma(2.0, 1.0),
            :lost => Gamma(5.0, 0.5))),
))

# Inspect tree, params, and default priors
event_tree(tree)
params_table(tree)
priors = build_priors(params_table(tree))

# Simulate a record and score it
rand(tree)
logpdf(tree, ans)
```

:::
:::

## Why compose distributions?

- **One object, two directions.** The same tree simulates records (`rand`) and
  scores them (`logpdf`), so a model is built once and used in both directions.
- **Named events.** Every delay in the tree has a label — `onset_admit`,
  `death`, `discharge` — so the record structure is self-documenting.
- **Flat parameters.** `params_table` inventories every free parameter in one
  table keyed by edge path, and `build_priors` derives a prior per parameter
  from its support.
- **Composable.** Trees nest: a `Resolve` node is a valid step in a
  `Sequential` chain, a `Parallel` set is built from a NamedTuple, and the five
  composers (`Sequential`, `Parallel`, `Resolve`, `Compete`, `Choose`) cover
  every event relationship.
- **Censoring is part of the tree.** Each leaf can be wrapped in
  `double_interval_censored` so the censoring is carried inside the composed
  object rather than handled separately — see the
  [CensoredDistributions tutorial](https://censoreddistributions.epiaware.org/dev/getting-started/tutorials/bdbv-linelist-analysis/).

## Where next

- Read the full BDBV line-list analysis at
  [CensoredDistributions.jl](https://censoreddistributions.epiaware.org/dev/getting-started/tutorials/bdbv-linelist-analysis/)
  — the north-star example for this approach.
- The [ComposedDistributions.jl documentation](https://composeddistributions.epiaware.org/dev/)
  covers all five composers, structural edits, and parameter tying.
- Ready to fit a full model? See the [assembled model approach](assembled-model.qmd).

::::::
